meta.title: Lev Lifecycle Map — Reduction + Platform Taxonomy + Absorption
meta.description: 3-part /now report: reduce Lev active skills, classify AXI/AgentFS/GitAgent/browser-tools by overlap type, absorb platform-skills into /ux and /tribunal

props.title: Reduce · Classify · Absorb
props.subtitle: Lev has 173 active skills. The 4-lane map says we only need 35. The batch items aren't skills — they're platforms with embedded skills. The skills get absorbed into /ux and /tribunal.

props.content: Three things happen on this page. (1) Lev reduces active skills from 173 → 35 using the lane map as the reduction axis. (2) AXI, AgentFS, GitAgent, agent-browser, dev-browser get a new classification — they're not skills, they're platforms with overlap. (3) Platform-embedded skills (TOON format, self-documenting --help, agentic UX patterns) get absorbed into existing Lev skills (/ux, /tribunal). The batch becomes smaller, smarter, and clearly typed.

props.label: PART 1
props.title: Reduce Lev: 173 → 35 Active Skills
props.subtitle: Map every active skill to a lane. Keep routers, Lev-native control surfaces, core tools. Move the rest.

props.content:
The 4-lane model is the reduction axis. Every skill answers one question: *which operator lane does this serve?* Skills that serve multiple lanes stay (routers). Skills that don't cleanly fit one lane get moved to `skills-db` where they remain resolvable via metadata lookup but don't clutter the active surface.

The reduction isn't deletion — every moved skill resolves via `skill://<name>` from metadata + top-level. The active surface is what agents see; skills-db is what agents can fetch on demand.

rows.skills: interview, cdo, prior-art, cass, cm, sherlock, lev-research, skill-discovery
arch, lev-plan, lev-align, lev-cdo, lev-roadmap, lev-sdlc, work
stack, lev-builder, lev-orch-sidequest, lev-intake, autodev-lev, codex-autoresearch, agent-browser, notionctl, sync, work-mvp
tribunal, ux, lev-design, lev-design-os, lev-cms, handoff, validate
skill-builder, using-superpowers, using-git-worktrees, prompt-guard, dcg, agent-field-kit, agentping, lev

props.title: Domain specialists
props.content: All `geo-*`, `testing-*`, most March 25 import-batch specialists. Platform-specific (Vercel, GA4, Stripe, iOS, etc.). Resolvable via skill-db metadata.

props.title: Narrow tools
props.content: Most cloud/github/platform wrappers. Media/render/testing specialists. Still resolvable via `skill://<name>`, just not in active surface.

props.title: Superseded
props.content: `brainstorming` (superseded by cdo/interview). `cass-daily-scan` (review before keep). Most rp-* (under review).

props.label: PART 2
props.title: Platform vs Lifecycle vs Skill
props.subtitle: AXI, AgentFS, GitAgent, agent-browser, dev-browser aren't lifecycle skills. They're platforms. But some have embedded skills.

props.content:
The Venn matrix showed 46 items classified as 'unlaned' — AXI, AgentFS, GitAgent, agent-browser, dev-browser. Calling them lifecycle skills is wrong. They're **platforms** (runtime infrastructure). But some of them have **embedded skills** — patterns, conventions, practices that can be extracted and reused.

Example: AXI is a platform (the runtime). Its TOON output format is a pattern. That pattern is a skill that can be absorbed into `/tribunal` (multi-model consensus benefits from token-efficient output). AXI's self-documenting `--help` is a pattern that gets absorbed into `/ux` (agentic UX design).

This introduces a new taxonomy dimension: **capability_type** = `pure-skill | pure-platform | platform-skill-hybrid`

props.title: 🎯 pure-skill
props.content:
**Lifecycle skill.** Maps cleanly to one lane. Operator-facing. Example: `prior-art` (Shape), `arch` (Plan), `stack` (Deliver), `tribunal` (Close).

These are THE 35 active skills.

props.title: 🏗️ pure-platform
props.content:
**Runtime infrastructure.** Not a skill, not operator-facing. Example: AgentFS (filesystem), agent-browser (browser tool), dev-browser (browser sandbox).

These live in platforms, not skills. They don't belong in `~/.agents/skills/`.

props.title: 🔀 platform-skill hybrid
props.content:
**Platform with embedded patterns.** The platform exists, but parts of it are reusable skills. Example: AXI (platform) has TOON format (skill), agentic UX patterns (skill), self-documenting --help (skill).

The patterns get **absorbed** into existing Lev skills.

rows.skill_part: TOON format, content-first output, contextual disclosure, --help = LLM guide, structured errors on stdout

rows.platform_part: Virtual FS, KV store, audit trail, mounts (FUSE/NFS/WASM/DO)
agent.yaml manifest, folder structure, framework exports
Rust CLI → Node daemon, 80+ commands, Playwright backend
rows.skill_part: Ref-based workflow pattern (absorb into agent-browser skill README)
Self-documenting CLI (absorb into /ux), fractal skill auto-generation (absorb into skill-builder)

props.label: PART 3
props.title: Absorb Into /ux and /tribunal
props.subtitle: 9 platform-skills absorbed. Originals stay as references; the patterns live in existing Lev skills.

props.content:
Absorption means: the platform's documentation stays authoritative, but the **pattern** becomes a named section inside a Lev skill's SKILL.md. When an operator calls `/ux`, they get the AXI agentic UX patterns automatically. When they call `/tribunal`, they get TOON format as the default output mode.

This is the opposite of fork-and-rewrite. The upstream platforms keep evolving; Lev cites the pattern and localizes the rationale.

props.title: 🎨 Absorb into /ux
props.content:
**Skill:** `~/.claude/skills/ux/SKILL.md`

**Absorb:**
- AXI `content-first CLI` — no-args returns live data not help
- AXI `contextual disclosure` — next-step templates after every response
- AXI `consistent --help per subcommand` — help is the LLM guide
- dev-browser `self-documenting CLI` — --help IS the guide

**Section to add:** 'Agentic CLI UX Patterns (from AXI + dev-browser)'

**Source attribution:** `.lev/pm/parity/axi.yaml`, `workshop/intake/dev-browser/`

props.title: ⚖️ Absorb into /tribunal
props.content:
**Skill:** `~/.claude/skills/tribunal/SKILL.md`

**Absorb:**
- AXI `TOON format` — 40% token savings vs JSON (79K vs 185K benchmarked)
- AXI `minimal default schemas` — cut defaults to essentials
- AXI `pre-computed aggregates` — counts/totals in response, not derived
- AXI `structured errors on stdout` — errors are data, not exceptions

**Section to add:** 'Token-Efficient Multi-Model Output (from AXI)'

**Why tribunal:** Multi-model consensus runs 3 agents — TOON format triples token savings. Critical for cost-effective parallel runs.

props.title: 1. Execute the move
props.content: The other skill-graph agent runs the active-skills-cap proposal. 173 → 35. Skills-db retains the rest, resolvable via metadata.

props.title: 2. Update graph schema
props.content: Add `capability_type` field to skill-graph nodes: `pure-skill | pure-platform | platform-skill-hybrid`. Re-seed from parity + inferred type.

props.title: 3. Ship absorptions
props.content: Edit `/ux/SKILL.md` and `/tribunal/SKILL.md` to add the absorbed pattern sections. Source attribution to parity files. No code changes — just skill docs.
